{
"article_title": "Improving Customer Service with Precog: A Deep Dive into Nubank's AI-Powered Routing System",
"article_summary": "This article discusses Nubank's Precog, an AI-powered routing system that improves customer service by accurately predicting the product a customer needs help with. The system uses contrastive learning to learn embeddings of events in a self-supervised manner and has resulted in significant decreases in time to solve issues and increases in customer satisfaction.",
"keywords": ["Nubank", "Precog", "AI", "customer service", "routing system", "contrastive learning", "embeddings", "self-supervised learning"],
"article_body": "In this article, we delve into Nubank's Precog, an AI-powered routing system that has revolutionized the way customer service is handled at the company. The system uses advanced machine learning techniques to predict the product a customer needs help with, thereby improving efficiency and customer satisfaction. 

Precog works by encoding events (e.g., app click stream, transactions) as a sequence of symbols. This allows the use of techniques originally developed for natural language processing, like embeddings and sequence models, to understand and predict customer needs. The richest source of data that was missing from Nubank's models was their app click stream, so that's where Precog started. 

The main challenge with this data is its lack of enforced structure across different product teams. Each record is identified by a metric name and a JSON with attribute/value pairs, all of which are determined by the engineers who implement each flow. In order to avoid development bottlenecks, there is no centralized governance, and these values can change quickly with each update of the app. 

To deal with this semi-structured data, Precog turns records into sequences of text-based identifiers, while dropping infrequent symbols, like unique ids. There are many possible ways of learning from these string representations, with different levels of complexity. Nubank decided to represent customers with a bag-of-words of their events’ symbols. 

Precog learns embeddings of the events in a self-supervised manner, using contrastive learning. A set of events from a customer is placed as the anchor, with a single event randomly removed from it to serve as a positive sample while other events that don’t occur for that customer are randomly sampled as negative. Contrastive learning tries to minimize the distance between the anchor and positive samples and at the same time maximize the distance to negative samples. At the end, a vector for each symbol in the vocabulary is produced and we aggregate them to get the customer representation. 

The system's main component is a pipeline to train events/customers embeddings and then incorporate the learned embeddings into downstream models at training and serving time. To train the embeddings, they use Starspace library, which provides a flexible and efficient implementation to learn any entity embeddings. 

To train a downstream model, they take records that are keyed by anonymous customer identifiers and timestamps, together with the labels they want to predict and any other features, and join them with the relevant sets of events. For example, in order to predict the contact reason for a customer support call, they take the events of that customer from the event window preceding the call and the contact reason as classified by agents. 

At run time, an event consumer microservice (implemented in their canonical business service language, Clojure) transforms the raw event data into a string format and stores it in a low-latency, temporary storage (Redis). At serving time, the downstream model microservice (built in Python) retrieves relevant events from the cache, computes embeddings, and uses them as features for a classification model. 

The first application of Precog is in routing of phone calls, through a downstream model that uses the customer embeddings and other features to rank the most likely product that the customer needs help with. If the model is confident enough, the system routes customers directly to specialists. Otherwise, they will talk to generalist agents, who may transfer them to specialists if they can’t handle the problem themselves. 

By adding Precog embeddings, they managed to increase the volume of calls that are correctly routed to specialized agents without need for customer input by more than 50%, compared to the previous model. This resulted in significant decreases in time to solve issues and increases in customer satisfaction. There were improvements for all the products they tested, but they noticed that the largest gains happened in newer products for which they hadn’t implemented traditional features yet. This confirms their hypothesis that they can replace laborious feature engineering with a more scalable approach. 

Another benefit of Precog is the reduction in latency. For traditional features, they had to call multiple REST services and process the results, which resulted in high worst-case latency. With Precog, the time to fetch and process event data is much lower and more predictable, resulting in lower overall serving latency. The freshness of the events (time between event happening and being available to serve) was a concern initially, but in practice they’re seeing them available almost instantly, making this approach very competitive with synchronous REST retrieval.",

The system uses contrastive learning to learn embeddings of events in a self-supervised manner and has resulted in significant decreases in time to solve issues and increases in customer satisfaction.",

{"output": "Here is a summary of the key points from the article: 

1. The article discusses an improved ETA (Estimated Time of Arrival) prediction model for delivery networks, specifically focusing on DoorDash's platform.

2. The new model uses an MLP-gated MoE (Multi-Layer Perceptron - Mixture of Experts) architecture to improve the expressiveness and learn various types of information automatically.

3. The MLP-gated MoE model employs three parallel encoders: a simple encoder, DeepNet encoder, CrossNet encoder, and Transformer encoder.

4. The simple encoder converts the input into a fixed dimension and normalizes feature values. The DeepNet encoder processes inputs through multiple layers, including numerical features, embeddings, and aggregated time series features. The CrossNet encoder models complexities and interdependencies between temporal/spatial/order features.

5. The Transformer encoder leverages the power of self-attention mechanisms to focus on modeling sequential dependencies and relationships.

6. Each encoder processes different input features, leading to comprehensive learning around various aspects of the information. The outputs from all encoders are combined into a single, rich representation, which is then fed into a multi-layer perceptron to generate probabilistic ETA predictions.

7. The new model also incorporates time series features to improve responsiveness to dynamic changes in the system and better adapt to changing conditions over time.", "source": "The article 'Understanding MLP-gated MoE architecture' from DoorDash Engineering Blog"}

# This is a Python script that demonstrates the use of LLMs (Large Language Models) to generate tests for mutation-guided test generation.

import re
import json

class EquivalenceDetector:
    def __init__(self, model):
        self.model = model

    def detect_equivalent(self, original_code, mutant_code):
        # Strip comments and whitespace for comparison
        original_code = re.sub(r'//.*|
', '', original_code)
        mutant_code = re.sub(r'//.*|
', '', mutant_code)

        # Check if the models predict that the two codes are equivalent
        result = self.model.predict([original_code, mutant_code], return_dict=True)
        return result['predictions'][0]['label'] == 'equal'

# Load the pre-trained LLM model

This state includes a lastPersistedTransaction and lastUpdatedTransaction number. When data changes, the embedding state's lastUpdatedTransaction is updated to reflect the current transaction. The system then generates tasks to re-embed the data if necessary, persists the embeddings in their vector database, and confirms with MemApp that the write has happened, updating the lastPersistedTransaction. {"base":"[{"name":"case","arguments":{}}]","head":["You are a model trained to analyze and summarize text data. Here is an article about using Fourier Transform for predicting defrost periods in refrigeration cases at Walmart.

The article explains that the challenge is to detect defrosts that are periodic in short term but slowly shifting on a longer time frame. To solve this, they use Fourier Transform to process the case temperature signal and extract defrost signal from it. The extracted defrost signal needs to be converted into a binary indicator for downstream modeling.

The model is tested against cases where the defrost command is accurate and readily available in IoT sensor data. The initial results are encouraging, with precision and recall being over 80% on average for cases where defrosts are regular. However, performance is low for cases where defrosts are aperiodic or lack general pattern.

The article also mentions that they will build on top of the current logic using a customized approach based on refrigeration case type, contents, and inputs from domain experts."],"body":["The article explains that to predict defrost periods in refrigeration cases at Walmart, they use Fourier Transform to process the case temperature signal and extract the defrost signal from it. The extracted defrost signal is then converted into a binary indicator for downstream modeling.

To do this, they first convert the case temperature signal from time domain into frequency domain using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). They identify that steady state temperature has zero frequency (dc signal) and highest magnitude, while defrost signal belongs to the frequency with second-highest magnitude. They then filter the frequency spectrum to contain only the frequency belonging to defrost and filter out the rest.

Next, they take Inverse Fourier Transform of the filtered frequency spectrum to get the defrost signal. The extracted defrost signal is converted into a binary indicator by thresholding it at a suitable point and classifying the upper portion as the defrost indicator.

The model is tested against cases where the defrost command is accurate and readily available in IoT sensor data. The initial results are encouraging, with precision and recall being over 80% on average for cases where defrosts are regular. However, performance is low for cases where defrosts are aperiodic or lack general pattern.

The article also mentions that they will build on top of the current logic using a customized approach based on refrigeration case type, contents, and inputs from domain experts."],"tail":["The model allows them to generate defrost commands for cases where the command is not readily available in data, predict future defrost schedules, and identify and correct historical defrost command for cases where defrost command in IoT data is inaccurate.

{"question":"What are the lessons learned about building an LLM-based product that actually works and ends up in production rather than languishing as a cool tech demo?", "answer":"1. You Do Not (Usually) Need an LLM: The first and most important step is a product one, not a technical one. Before diving into using LLMs, think carefully about whether it's necessary for your product. If you have a problem that benefits from LLMs where you can mitigate the inevitable drawbacks, and you can design the right interface and cost model to make it work, then you can create the future. 

2. Build a Robust Evaluation Framework: Even when you mitigate randomness, you still need to ensure consistent quality of results. You should build a multi-layered evaluation system to manage this. This includes automated validation, expert review by a person, and storing all evaluations to build training data for future improvements.

3. Design the Right Interface: The association between LLMs and chat interfaces is extremely strong; however, it has unfortunately become the standard for most models. Consider other types of solutions that might be more suitable for your use case.

4. Cost Model: As of December 2024, the cost of running a powerful LLM is still a few dollars per million tokens and returns full results in tens of seconds. This is too slow and expensive for many consumer applications. You should design a cost model that works for your specific use case.

5. Continuous Improvement: Regular prompt refinement, based on accumulated feedback, helps continuously enhance the system’s performance. Use templates to re-run your meal generations for new prompts and models easily."}

The AI feature was tested in two phases. In the first phase, 75% of the user base was assigned to the treatment group, where they could opt to use the AI feature, while the other 25% formed the control group and were not exposed to the feature. However, the primary success metric, which measures the percentage of activities that get submitted out of the ones that start the product creation wizard, was significantly lower than expected in the treatment group. This was due to user confusion and a lack of trust in the AI tool. The UI didn't explain what the automatic content creator was or what it was helping them do. Additionally, activity providers in the treatment group were spending longer on the pages that were not filled out by AI, as they were frustrated about having to fill out some of the pages manually.

In the second phase, improvements were made to the UI and the LLM to improve the content quality and to automatically fill out more sections. The UI improvements reduced user drop-off from the AI input page to the following page in product creation by 5 percentage points. Users also had similar times spent on non-AI-assisted pages of product creation, so the time on these manual pages was decreased from the first experiment (due to users having been informed what was coming and knowing what they would have to do themselves).

The end result of the second phase was an increase in all success metrics. By assisting activity onboarding, activity providers were more likely to complete onboarding for their activities, and the higher quality content meant we saw a solid increase in activity performance for activities onboarded through AI. The feature was then launched to 100% of the user base.

Key learnings from this project include having perseverance in your convictions, being data-driven, collaborative communication, maintaining central knowledge repositories and singular source of truth databases, early analytics involvement for new product features, expecting the unexpected and paying meticulous attention to detail, outliers are insightful, expectation setting shapes user behavior, anticipating potential issues before they arise, and setting up monitoring systems to track them.

Grammarly Research: Multilingual Editing with Large Language Models
==============================================================

In this paper, we present mEdIT (Multilingual Editing with Instruction Tuning), a method for training large language models (LLMs) to accept edit instructions in one language and perform those edits in several other languages. We demonstrate that with careful fine-tuning, these LLMs perform much better than all other models built to date, and receive positive feedback from human evaluators.

Our approach involves fine-tuning pre-trained multilingual LLMs on a large dataset of instruction-text pairs in multiple languages. The fine-tuned models are then evaluated on several editing tasks, including grammar correction, simplification, and paraphrasing, across six language families: English, German, Chinese, Spanish, Arabic, Japanese, Korean, and Hindi.

We find that the fine-tuned LLMs generalize well to languages they haven't been specifically fine-tuned for, and perform at a high level no matter what language the instructions are in or whether the language matches the body of the text. We also observe that model size is crucial, with larger models showing significantly improved performance across all tasks.

We make our work and data publicly available on GitHub and Hugging Face to foster further inquiry into the topic. We also encourage interested readers to check out open roles on Grammarly's Strategic Research team if they are passionate about exploring and leveraging the potential of LLMs and generative AI to improve writing and communication for everyone.
